name: The Hassan Foundation
mission: TO PROMOTE HUMAN DEVELOPMENT BY PRESERVING LIFE THROUGH IMPROVING HEALTH AND EDUCATION AMONGST THE POOR PEOPLE IN PAKISTAN. TO PROVIDE CLEAN POTABLE WAT...
theoryOfChange: Provision of clean potable water to underserved communities to reduce illness and deaths from contaminated water and to reduce the burden of traveling long distances for water; support for education and vocational training as additional longer-term support.
scoreSummary: The Hassan Foundation shows good impact and good alignment, with zakat compliance (minor risk deductions applied).

keyConcerns.headline: Zakat-collecting organization holds 100 months of reserves
keyConcerns.detail: Zakat must be distributed promptly per Islamic jurisprudence. Large reserves raise concerns about timely distribution.

judge_issues.message: The rationale mentions high financial efficiency and direct service model as strengths, but it doesn't adequately address the significant deduction due to the excessive working capital (99.7 months), which is a key factor in tempering the overall score. The rationale should also mention the risk deduction due to the zakat compliance issue.

While it provides direct services like safe water and education, the low data confidence score reflects a lack of extensive third-party evidence regarding specific outcomes [3].

The organization is Zakat-eligible, maintaining a specific policy to ensure donations reach those in need according to religious guidelines [4].

strengths.detail: The charity directs <cite id="4">88.2% of its total expenses</cite> toward program services, significantly exceeding the <cite id="11">75% industry standard</cite>.
The foundation maintains a <cite id="10">fundraising efficiency of $0.00 per $1 raised</cite>, ensuring donor contributions are not eroded by marketing costs.
With <cite id="7">over 99.7 months of working capital</cite>, the organization possesses a rare level of long-term fiscal sustainability.

strengths_deep_dive: The Hassan Foundation demonstrates a commitment to direct impact by maintaining a <cite id="4">88.2% program expense ratio</cite>, which is higher than the <cite id="11">peer median of 90.3%</cite>. This efficiency is supported by a <cite id="12">100% independent board</cite> that oversees the distribution of <cite id="6">$712,272 in international grants</cite>. Their focus on <cite id="3">clean water and education</cite> in Pakistan addresses critical infrastructure gaps in underserved regions.
rich_narrative.summary: The Hassan Foundation is a faith-based humanitarian organization that has focused on <cite id="1">preserving life through health and education</cite> in Pakistan since its <cite id="2">founding in 2007</cite>. The charity operates primarily as a direct service provider, specializing in <cite id="3">clean water projects, orphan support, and disaster relief</cite>. While the organization maintains a <cite id="4">high program expense ratio of 88.2%</cite>, it currently faces financial headwinds, with <cite id="5">revenue declining 21%</cite> in the most recent fiscal year. Despite this contraction, the foundation remains a significant grantmaker for international relief, distributing <cite id="6">$712,272 in foreign grants</cite> to 12 recipients in 2022. A defining characteristic of the foundation is its extreme financial conservatism; it currently holds <cite id="7">99.7 months of working capital</cite> in working capital. This level of liquidity provides immense stability but raises questions regarding the speed of capital deployment for urgent humanitarian needs. The organization is <cite id="8">zakat-eligible</cite>, specifically serving the <cite id="9">fuqara (poor) and masakin (needy)</cite> categories of asnaf. Its operations are lean, reporting <cite id="10">$0.00 spent to raise every $1</cite> in public contributions.
